---
title: "Is gymflation real?"
subtitle: "Investigating with R and LLMs"
author: "Chris Bowdon"
institution: "CTO at Polecat Intelligence Ltd"
format: 
    revealjs:
        incremental: true
        theme: serif
        css: slides.css
        slide-number: true
        progress: true
execute: 
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

# Premise

## What is _gymflation_?

Gymflation is the idea that social media is increasing the perceived average strength level.

::: aside
Blog post with all the code and data: [https://cbowdon.github.io/posts/gymflation](https://cbowdon.github.io/posts/gymflation)
:::

## What is _gymflation_?

- An excuse to show you some LLM techniques.
- A phenomenon connected to social media's negative effects on self-image, which is increasingly leading to mental health issues such as body dysmorphia.
- Mostly the former though.

## Comparison to superhero inflation {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=50%}
![Batman demonstrates the related idea "superhero inflation".](./img/batman_physique.jpg)
:::
::: {.column width=50%}
"When I grow up, I want to be as strong as Batman" is less achievable now than it was in the 60s.
:::
::::

# Plan

## How can we investigate this?

Measure: are feats of strength reported in online videos truly increasing?

Taking a very narrow (achievable) measure: deadlift videos on YouTube.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="30%"}
![](./img/example_bottom.jpg)
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
![](./img/example_mid.jpg)
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
![](./img/example_top.jpg)
:::

::::


## Steps

- Scrape the YouTube API for 10 years of deadlift personal record (PR) videos with httr2
- Use structured responses to extract the lifted weight with an LLM
- Evaluate the quality of our extraction and improve it with In-Context Learning (ICL)
- Use a multimodal model to judge the lifter's gender
- Make extensive use of caching so errors don't set us back
- Plot our results with good old ggplot2

# Data gathering

## YouTube API

We can use httr2 for this.

```{r}
#| label: youtube
library(httr2)

req <- request("https://youtube.googleapis.com/youtube/v3/search") |>
  req_cache(".cache/httr2") |> # <1>
  req_url_query(
    part = "snippet",
    q = query,
    type = "video",
    maxResults = 50,
    duration = "short",
    region = "UK",
    published_after = published_after,
    published_before = published_before,
    key = YOUTUBE_API_KEY
  ) |>
  req_headers(Accept = "application/json") |>
  req_timeout(30)

req_perform(req) |>
  resp_body_json(simplifyVector = TRUE) # <2>
```

1. Client-side caching saves you from wasting time or request quota.
2. Getting data back as a dataframe is very convenient.

## Data

```{r}
#| label: tbl-raw-data
#| tbl-cap: Sample of the raw data from the YouTube API.
#| cap-location: bottom
#| eval: true
#| echo: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
df <- readRDS("data/youtube_df.rds")
df |>
  slice_sample(n = 5) |>
  select(
    id.videoId,
    publishedAt,
    channelTitle,
    title,
    description,
    thumbnails.high.url
  ) |>
  kable()
```

# Extraction model

## First model

## Improving our model with In-Context Learning (ICL)

# First look at the data

## Image classification

## Model search

## Second look at the data

## Summing up
